note: Life-cycle-spanning eval suite: each case tests the skill's diagnosis-driven reasoning at a different stage (young loss-maker, high-growth compounder, financial-firm guard, mature low-exposure exporter, cyclical commodity, complex conglomerate). Inputs are embedded so both skill versions work from identical authentic data, isolating calibration from scraping luck.

evals.name: zomato-young-lossmaker
evals.prompt: Build me a story-driven DCF and tell me what Zomato is worth per share at its 2021 IPO, and whether the ₹76 IPO price is justified. Use ONLY these sourced figures (₹ Crore), do not scrape anything: FY21 revenue ₹1,994 Cr; the company is loss-making (FY21 net loss ~₹816 Cr; operating margin deeply negative today, roughly −40%). Post-IPO net CASH ≈ ₹14,000 Cr (it raised ₹9,000 Cr fresh in the IPO and holds it). Diluted shares ≈ 785 Cr. It is an Indian company. Story inputs you can use: India food-delivery TAM ≈ ₹22,500 Cr in 2021, plausibly growing to ~₹2,00,000 Cr over 10 years; Zomato's market share ~40%; take-rate ~22% of gross order value; a profitable food-delivery business in steady state earns roughly a 30% pre-tax operating margin once selling costs scale down. India 10y G-sec ~7%. Give me a fair-value RANGE, a margin of safety vs ₹76, and your honest read. Save the report and inputs under ./artifacts/.

assertions.text: Produces a concrete positive intrinsic value per share (does NOT refuse/defer to relative valuation just because the company is loss-making)
Reports a fair-value RANGE rather than a single point estimate
Base/central per-share value lands in a Damodaran-consistent band, roughly ₹30–₹55 (his base was ₹41, MC median ₹36)
Applies an explicit probability-of-failure / distress haircut for the young loss-maker
Makes country risk explicit in the discount rate (India ERP/country-risk premium, ₹ cost of equity ~13–15%) rather than a single low guessed WACC
Builds revenue from TAM × market share × take-rate (top-down), not a growth rate pulled from nowhere
Concludes ₹76 is not justified by the base story while acknowledging the right-tail/optionality could reach it (matches Damodaran's actual read)

evals.name: structural-grower-priced-rich
evals.prompt: Value this stock for me and tell me if it's overvalued at ₹1,250. Use ONLY these sourced figures (₹ Crore), don't scrape: it's a niche capital-goods leader, FY26 revenue ₹1,200 Cr, and it has compounded revenue at ~34% CAGR over the last 5 years. EBIT margin 24% and slowly rising; ROCE ~28%; net CASH ₹60 Cr; diluted shares 1.2 Cr; current price ₹1,250 (so ~60x earnings). Management guides 25–30% revenue growth for the next 3–4 years and the order book supports it; the addressable market is large and it is the clear leader. India 10y G-sec ~7%. I keep seeing DCFs that say this kind of stock is worth 60–70% less than the market price and I don't trust them — give me an honest valuation with a real range and tell me whether the gap is overvaluation or model conservatism. Save report + inputs under ./artifacts/.

assertions.text: Reports a value RANGE driven by growth rate AND growth duration / steady-state margin (the story levers), not only a WACC×terminal-growth grid
Runs a reverse DCF and compares the price-implied growth to the company's DEMONSTRATED ~34% CAGR / management guidance
Explicitly distinguishes overvaluation from base-case conservatism (premature growth fade), per the user's actual question
Does not mechanically fade demonstrated 34% growth to GDP by year 10 without justification; considers a longer high-growth runway / 15y window
Uses an India-appropriate discount rate (~13–15% ₹ cost of equity / country risk) and a terminal growth near the rupee risk-free (~6.5–7%), not 4%

evals.name: nbfc-dcf-fragile-guard
evals.prompt: What's this NBFC worth — build me a DCF and tell me if it's overvalued at ₹600? Sourced figures: a retail-lending NBFC, AUM ₹40,000 Cr growing ~22% a year, RoE ~17%, book value per share ₹190, current price ₹600 (so ~3.2x book), diluted shares ~62 Cr. It funds itself with borrowings. Save your analysis under ./artifacts/.

assertions.text: States that an FCFF DCF is ill-defined / fragile for a lender (debt is raw material, not financing) and does NOT force a standard FCFF DCF as the primary answer
Leans on the appropriate lender framework instead — P/B vs RoE, excess-return / residual-income, or cost-of-equity-discounted equity FCFE
Still gives the user a usable valuation read and verdict vs ₹600 rather than just refusing

evals.name: mature-it-exporter-lambda
evals.prompt: Value this IT services company and tell me if it's overvalued at ₹1,800. ONLY these sourced figures (₹ Crore), don't scrape: FY26 revenue ₹38,000 Cr, of which ~95% comes from exports (US/Europe clients) and only ~5% from India. EBIT margin ~23%, stable for years; RoE ~27%; net CASH ₹8,000 Cr; diluted shares 180 Cr; pays steady dividends; revenue grew ~9%/yr over 5 years, management guides 7–9%. It is a mature industry leader. India 10y G-sec ~7%; US 10y ~4.3%. Give a fair-value range and an honest read.

assertions.text: Loads India country risk by OPERATING EXPOSURE (low lambda, ~0.1), not by incorporation — recognising a 95%-export firm barely carries India's CRP
Diagnoses a mature stage and does NOT apply a failure/distress probability to a profitable cash-generative firm
Sets terminal growth and growth path appropriate to a mature firm (fade to ~economy), constrained by its demonstrated ~9% history
Cost of equity reflects the low India exposure (materially below a full-India-CRP number), and the report explains why

evals.name: cyclical-commodity-normalize
evals.prompt: Value this metals/commodity producer and tell me if it's overvalued at ₹600. ONLY these figures (₹ Crore), don't scrape: FY26 revenue ₹60,000 Cr, but profitability swings hard with commodity prices — EBIT margin was ~8% in the last downcycle, ~22% at the peak, and is ~18% now near a cyclical high. Net DEBT ₹25,000 Cr; diluted shares 380 Cr; RoCE has swung between 6% and 20% across the cycle; India-heavy operations. G-sec ~7%. Give an honest valuation and read.

assertions.text: NORMALISES margins/returns through the cycle rather than capitalising the current near-peak ~18% margin
States DCF is fragile for a commodity/cyclical firm and that the commodity-price call is the real swing factor (makes it explicit), leaning on normalised + relative valuation
Still delivers a usable valuation read and verdict vs ₹600 rather than refusing

evals.name: complex-conglomerate-governance
evals.prompt: Value this infrastructure conglomerate and tell me if it's overvalued at ₹2,800. ONLY these figures (₹ Crore), don't scrape: FY26 revenue ₹90,000 Cr growing ~25%/yr, but EBIT margin is only ~6% and ROIC has fallen toward ~4% even as the group scaled ~8x; growth funded almost entirely by debt (net DEBT ₹70,000 Cr); the promoter family holds ~73% with significant share pledging and an opaque web of related-party transactions and cross-holdings; many assets are politically-linked infrastructure concessions. Diluted shares 110 Cr. G-sec ~7%. Give an honest valuation and read.

assertions.text: Values the OPERATIONS cleanly first, then applies a SEPARATE explicit complexity/governance discount (the Adani method) — does NOT bury governance inside a gloomy growth rate
Flags that ROIC (~4%) sits below the cost of capital, so scaling/growth destroys rather than creates value (NO_TERMINAL_EXCESS_RETURN reasoning)
Addresses the leverage / distress and pledging risk (failure or distress consideration), not just the operating cash flows
Concludes ₹2,800 is unjustified even before/independent of governance, then larger discount for control complexity (matches Damodaran's Adani read)
